Suunnittele 100 km:n pyörälenkki Haminasta

header.user-agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; CrOS x86_64 14541.0.0) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/114.0.0.0 Safari/537.36

conversation.content:
Tässä on ehdotus 100 km:n pyörälenkille Haminasta. Reitti kulkee kauniiden järvimaisemien, pikkukylien ja historiallisten nähtävyyksien läpi. Reitin varrella on mahdollisuus pysähtyä tauolle esimerkiksi kahviloissa, luontokohteissa ja uimarannoilla. Voit sopeuttaa reittiä omiin mieltymyksiinne ja tarpeisiinne.

1. Aloita matka Haminan keskustasta, jossa voi ihailla ainutlaatuista ympyräkaavaa ja historiallisia rakennuksia.

2. Aja etelään noin 11 km pitkin Kymintietä (tie nro 170) kohti Pyterlahtea, joka sijaitsee Itämeren rannalla. Nauti merimaisemista ja mahdollisesti pysähdy Pyterlahden uimarannalla vilvoittelemaan.

3. Käänny Pyterlahden jälkeen pohjoiseen ja aja noin 6 km pitkin Pyterlahdentietä (tie nro 3542) kohti Virolaa.

4. Virolasta jatka pohjoiseen ja seuraa Vanhamaantietä (tie nro 356) noin 14 km pitkin kohti Summaa. Matkan varrella voit ihailla Suomen vaihtelevaa järvi- ja metsämaisemaa.

5. Summassa voit pysähtyä hetkeksi tutustumaan Suomenlinnan Summanlinnan vallitusjäännöksiin, jotka muodostavat osan Suomen sodanaikaisista linnoituksista.

6. Summassa käänny länteen ja aja Klamilaan. Seuraa Klamilantietä (tie nro 355) noin 13 km pitkin kohti Klamilaa. Kylässä voit kenties tehdä evästauon tai nauttia kupin kahvia paikallisessa kahvilassa.

7. Klamilasta suuntaa luoteeseen Virojoelle pitkin Virojoentietä (tie nro 357). Aja noin 15 km, kunnes saavut Virojoelle. Virojoen kirkonkylällä on kauppa, josta voi ostaa eväitä tai täydentää vesivarastoja.

8. Virojoelta jatka länteen ja seuraa Virolahdentietä (tie nro 3571) noin 8 km Virolahden kautta kohti Miehikkälää. Ajomatkan voi taittaa sivu- tai pikkuteitä pitkin ja samalla nauttia Suomen maalaismaisemista.

9. Miehikkälässä pysähdy vierailemaan Salpalinja-museossa, joka kertoo mielenkiintoisia tarinoita Suomen sotahistoriasta.

10. Miehikkälästä aja itään noin 23 km pitkin Miehikkäläntietä (tie nro 387) ja Summanjokilaaksoa kohti Virolaa. Matkan varrella voit ihailla koskemattomia metsä- ja jokimaisemia.

11. Virolasta jatka matkaa pitkin Pyterlahdentietä (tie nro 3542) noin 6 km Haminan suuntaan.

12. Saavuttuasi takaisin Pyterlahteen voit vielä nauttia merellisestä maisemasta, ennen kuin paluu Kymintietä (tie nro 170) pitkin Haminan keskustaan, noin 11 km.

Tämä 100 km:n pyörälenkki on vain yksi esimerkki mahdollisesta reitistä. Varmista ennen matkaa, että polkupyöräsi on hyvässä kunnossa, pakkaa mukaan riittävästi juomista ja eväitä, sekä muista liikkua turvallisesti liikenteessä.

i have a c# software and i want it to run every 3 hours and i dont want to use my own computer also dont have any vps, is there any free online service to run my exe every 3 hours?

header.user-agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/113.0.0.0 Safari/537.36

conversation.content:
Unfortunately, there is no completely free service that allows you to run a C# executable every 3 hours. However, you can use some low-cost or free-tier services like Azure Functions or AWS Lambda. 

1. Azure Functions: Microsoft’s Azure Functions offers a consumption plan that provides you 1 million free requests and 400,000 GB-s of resource consumption per month. You can create a timer-triggered Azure Function and run your C# code within it. You can follow the pricing details on their website to get the free tier: https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/details/functions/

2. AWS Lambda: Amazon Web Services (AWS) also provides a basic free tier with 1 million free requests per month and 400,000 GB-s of compute time per month. You can create an AWS Lambda function and trigger it using Amazon EventBridge at an interval of 3 hours. AWS Lambda supports dotnet core runtime. Check the pricing detail at https://aws.amazon.com/lambda/pricing/.

Both these services require you to set up a timer trigger in their respective ecosystems for running your code every 3 hours, but they provide an easy way to get started with serverless applications and execute your code.

Singular value decomposition
This exercise continues with a more challenging task in numerical programming. That is, we ask you to implement a method that computes a singular value decomposition (SVD) of a given (real) matrix.
In more precise terms, given an m × n matrix A with real entries as input, you are asked to compute a decomposition A = UΣVT where
1. U is an m × m real orthogonal matrix (UUT = I where I is the m × m identity matrix),
2. Σ is an m x n nonnegative (rectangular) diagonal matrix, and
3. V is an n x n real orthogonal matrix (VVT = I where I is the n x n identity matrix).
The three-tuple (U, Σ, V) of matrices is a singular value decomposition (SVD) of the matrix A.
Remark. To simplify the task you can assume that m, n ≤ 20. Furthermore, we allow your code to be numerically quite sloppy. Namely, if A has independent and identically distributed Gaussian entries (zero mean, unit variance), your code should return with high probability an SVD (U, E, V) such that the properties (i) A = UΣVT, (ii) UUT = I, (iii) Σ is nonnegative rectangular diagonal, and (iv) VVT = I all hold within entrywise additive error of € = 106. In practice this holds if your code passes the unit
tests.
The singular value decomposition enables easy access to many basic properties of a matrix such as its inverse, pseudoinverse, null space, range, low-rank approximations, and so forth. (See here for more.)
In the context of statistics and machine learning, the singular value decomposition is used in, for example, principal component analysis.

Hints
To compute the SVD of A it is sufficient to solve eigenvector/eigenvalue problems for the real symmetric matrices A transpose(A) and transepose(A) A.
Algorithms such as the QR algorithm or the Jacobi algorithm can be used to solve for eigenvectors and eigenvalues.

Use Scala 3. Implement the function def svd: (Matrix, Matrix, Matrix) Replace the symbol "???" with your solution. Do not use external libraries like breeze.

package svd

/* A simple class for matrix arithmetic. */

class Matrix(val m: Int, val n: Int):
  require(m > 0, "The dimension m must be positive")
  require(n > 0, "The dimension n must be positive")
  protected[Matrix] val entries = new Array[Double](m * n)

  /* Convenience constructor for square matrices */
  def this(n: Int) = this(n, n)

  /* Access the elements of a matrix Z by writing Z(i,j) */
  def apply(row: Int, column: Int) =
    require(0 <= row && row < m)
    require(0 <= column && column < n)
    entries(row * n + column)
  end apply

  /* Set the elements by writing Z(i,j) = v */
  def update(row: Int, column: Int, value: Double) =
    require(0 <= row && row < m)
    require(0 <= column && column < n)
    entries(row * n + column) = value
  end update

  /* Gives a string representation */
  override def toString =
    val s = new StringBuilder()
    for row <- 0 until m do
      for column <- 0 until n do
        s ++= " %f".format(this(row,column))
      s ++= "\n"
    s.toString
  end toString

  /* Returns the transpose of this matrix */
  def transpose =
    val result = new Matrix(n,m)
    for row <- 0 until m; column <- 0 until n do
      result(column, row) = this(row, column)
    result
  end transpose   

  /* Returns a new matrix that is the sum of this and that */
  def +(that: Matrix) =
    require(m == that.m && n == that.n)
    val result = new Matrix(m,n)
    (0 until m*n).foreach(i => { 
      result.entries(i) = this.entries(i) + that.entries(i) 
    })
    result
  end +

  /* Returns a new matrix that negates the entries of this */
  def unary_- =
    val result = new Matrix(m,n)
    (0 until m*n).foreach(i => { 
      result.entries(i) = -entries(i)
    })
    result
  end unary_-

  /* Returns a new matrix that is the difference of this and that */
  def -(that: Matrix) = this + -that

  /* Returns a new matrix that is the product of 'this' and 'that' */
  def *(that: Matrix): Matrix =
    require(n == that.m)
    val thatT = that.transpose  // transpose 'that' to get better cache-locality
    def inner(r1: Int, r2: Int) =
      var s = 0.0
      var i = 0
      while i < n do
        // the inner loop -- here is where transposing 'that' pays off
        s = s + entries(r1+i)*thatT.entries(r2+i) 
        i = i+1
      s
    val result = new Matrix(m,that.n)
    (0 until m*that.n).foreach(i => { result.entries(i) = inner((i/that.n)*n, (i%that.n)*n) })
    result
  end *

  /* 
   * YOUR TASK:  
   * Implement the following method that returns the singular value
   * decomposition of this matrix.
   *
   */

  /* Returns the singular value decomposition of this matrix */
  def svd: (Matrix, Matrix, Matrix) =
    ???
  end svd

end Matrix

object Matrix:
  val r = new util.Random(1234567)

  def rand(m: Int, n: Int) =
    val a = new Matrix(m,n)
    for i <- 0 until m; j <- 0 until n do { a(i,j) = r.nextDouble() }
    a

  def randGaussian(m: Int, n: Int) =
    val a = new Matrix(m,n)
    for i <- 0 until m; j <- 0 until n do { a(i,j) = r.nextGaussian() }
    a

  def identity(n: Int) =
    val a = new Matrix(n)
    (0 until n).foreach(i => a(i,i) = 1)
    a
end Matrix

Fix tasks 5, 6 and 7: package transactions:

  import org.apache.spark.rdd.RDD
  import org.apache.spark.SparkContext

  import java.util.Locale
  import java.util.TimeZone
  import java.text.SimpleDateFormat


  @main def main(): Unit =

    /*
     * Let us start by setting up a Spark context which runs locally
     * using two worker threads.
     *
     * Here we go:
     *
     */

    val sc = new SparkContext("local[2]", "transactions")

    /*
    * The following setting controls how ``verbose'' Spark is.
    * Comment this out to see all debug messages.
    * Warning: doing so may generate massive amount of debug info,
    * and normal program output can be overwhelmed!
    */
    sc.setLogLevel("WARN") 

    /*
     * Next, let us set up our input. 
     */

    val path = "a02-transactions/data/"
    /*
     * After the path is configured, we need to set up the input
     * files. This exercise has two input files.
     *
     */

    val filename_balances     = path ++ "ACMEBank-simulated-balances-2013-12-31-end-of-day.txt"
    val filename_transactions = path ++ "ACMEBank-simulated-transactions-from-2014-01-01-to-2014-12-31-inclusive.txt"

    /*
     * Let us now set up RDDs for the balances and transactions.
     *
     */

    val lines_balances     : RDD[String] = sc.textFile(filename_balances)
    val lines_transactions : RDD[String] = sc.textFile(filename_transactions)

    /* The following requirement sanity-checks the number of lines in the files 
     * -- if this requirement fails you are in trouble.
     */

    require(lines_balances.count() == 10000 &&
            lines_transactions.count() == 1000000)

    /*
     * Now parse the balances to a more convenient RDD
     * consisting of pairs (i,b) where i is an account identifier
     * and b is the start-of-year balance (in euro cents). 
     *
     */

    val balances : RDD[(String,Long)] = 
       lines_balances.map(line => { val ib = line.split(" +"); (ib(0),ib(1).toLong) } )

    /*
     * Next let us set up an RDD for the transcations. This requires 
     * a somewhat more intricate parser because we are converting the
     * __timestamps__ given in UTC (Coordinated Universal Time) in the data
     * into an internal binary representation as a Long, namely the number 
     * of milliseconds since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00:000 UTC.
     *
     */

    /* 
     * Set up a time stamp formatter using the Java date formatters.
     * https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/11/docs/api/java.base/java/text/SimpleDateFormat.html
     */

    val stamp_fmt = new SimpleDateFormat("EEE dd MMM yyyy HH:mm:ss:SSS zzz", Locale.UK)
    stamp_fmt.setTimeZone(TimeZone.getTimeZone("UTC"))
    val start_inclusive_d = stamp_fmt.parse("Wed 01 Jan 2014 00:00:00:000 UTC")
    // val end_exclusive_d   = stamp_fmt.parse("Thu 01 Jan 2015 00:00:00:000 UTC")
    val start_inclusive = start_inclusive_d.getTime() // first millisec in 2014
    // val end_exclusive   = end_exclusive_d.getTime()   // first millisec in 2015
    // val millis_2014 = end_exclusive - start_inclusive // # millisecs in 2014

    /*
     * Let us create a transaction RDD consisting of 4-tuples with the following
     * structure:
     *
     * 1) the time stamp (a Long between 'start_inclusive' and 'end_exclusive')
     * 2) the source account identifier (e.g. "A1234")
     * 3) the target account identifier (e.g. "A1234")
     * 4) the transferred amount (in euro cents)
     *
     */

    val stamp_len = "Wed 01 Jan 2014 00:00:00:000 UTC".length

    val transactions : RDD[(Long,String,String,Long)] = 
       lines_transactions.map(line => { 
         val stamp = line.take(stamp_len)
         val rest = line.drop(stamp_len+1).split(" +")
         (stamp_fmt.parse(stamp).getTime(), // timestamp
          rest(0),                          // source id
          rest(1),                          // target id
          rest(2).toLong                    // amount transferred
         ) 
       } )

    /* 
     * Our input is now set up. Now it is time for you to step in!
     *
     */

    /*
     * Task 1:
     * Find the identifier of the account with the largest balance at 
     * end-of-day, December 31, 2013. 
     *
     * Write the CODE that finds the identifier below. 
     * Once you have the VALUE of the identifier, save it in "transactionsSolutions.scala".
     *
     */

    val task1: String = balances.reduce((a, b) => if (a._2 > b._2) a else b)._1

    println("OUTPUT: The identifier for Task 1 is \"%s\"".format(task1))


    /*
     * Task 2:
     * Find the total volume (total amount of money
     * transferred in all the transactions), in euro cents.
     *
     * Write the CODE that finds the volume below. 
     * Once you have the VALUE of the volume, save it in "transactionsSolutions.scala".
     *
     */

    val task2: Long = transactions.map(_._4).sum().toLong

    println("OUTPUT: The volume for Task 2 is %d".format(task2))


    /*
     * Task 3:
     * Find the identifier of the account with the largest balance at 
     * end-of-day, December 31, 2014. 
     *
     * Write the CODE that finds the identifier below. 
     * Once you have the VALUE of the identifier, save it in "transactionsSolutions.scala".
     *
     * Hints: Key with account identifies, take union of initial balances
     *        and transactions. Reduce by key. Each transaction decreases 
     *        the balance of the source account and increases the balance 
     *        of the target account. All transactions have distinct time 
     *        stamps. 
     * 
     *
     */

    val initialBalances: RDD[(String, Long)] = balances.union(transactions.map(t => (t._2, 0L))).distinct()
    val updatedBalances: RDD[(String, Long)] = transactions
      .flatMap(t => Seq((t._2, -t._4), (t._3, t._4)))
      .reduceByKey(_ + _)
    val task3: String = initialBalances
      .join(updatedBalances)
      .mapValues(t => t._1 + t._2)
      .reduce((a, b) => if (a._2 > b._2) a else b)
      ._1


    println("OUTPUT: The identifier for Task 3 is \"%s\"".format(task3))


    /*
     * Task 4:
     * Find the number of idle accounts (that is, accounts that do
     * not take part in any transaction) in the year 2014.
     *
     * Write the CODE that finds the number of accounts below.
     * Once you have the VALUE, save it in "transactionsSolutions.scala".
     *
     */

    val task4: Long = balances
    .leftOuterJoin(transactions.flatMap(t => Seq((t._2, 1), (t._3, 1))))
    .filter { case (_, (balance, transactions)) => balance > 0 && transactions.isEmpty }
    .count()


    println("OUTPUT: The number of accounts for Task 4 is %d".format(task4))


    /*
     * Task 5:
     * The year 2014 can be partitioned into exactly 365*24 = 8760 
     * consecutive hours. Each hour lasts 3600000 milliseconds. 
     * That is, in terms of time stamps, the first hour of 2014 starts 
     * with the time stamp 1388534400000 and ends with the time 
     * stamp 1388537999999. The second hour of 2014 starts with the time 
     * stamp 1388538000000 and ends with the time stamp 1388541599999. 
     * And so forth, until we are at the last hour of 2014, which starts 
     * with the time stamp 1420066800000 and ends with the time stamp 
     * 1420070399999. Find the number of hours in 2014 during which the
     * total volume of transactions is at least ten million euro
     * (at least one billion euro cents). 
     *
     * Write the CODE that finds the number of hours below.
     * Once you have the VALUE, save it in "transactionsSolutions.scala".
     * 
     */ 

    val task5: Long = transactions
    .filter { case (_, _, _, amount) => amount >= 100000000 } // Filter transactions with at least one billion euro cents
    .map { case (timestamp, _, _, _) => timestamp / 3600000 } // Map the timestamp to hours
    .distinct() // Get distinct hours
    .count() // Count the number of distinct hours


    println("OUTPUT: The number of hours for Task 5 is %d".format(task5))


    /*
     * Task 6 (more challenging):
     *
     * Find the number of accounts whose balance is at least 
     * one million euro (one hundred million euro cents) 
     * at any time instant during the year 2014.
     * Note that these time instants may be different for 
     * different accounts.
     *
     * Write the CODE that finds the number of accounts below.
     * Once you have the VALUE, save it in "transactionsSolutions.scala".
     *
     * Hints: group by identifier (mind your memory!), 
     *        order by timestamp. All transactions have distinct 
     *        timestamps. 
     *
     */

    val balancesMap: Map[String, Long] = balances.collect().toMap

    val task6: Long = transactions
      .flatMap { case (_, from, to, _) => Seq(from, to) } // Create a sequence of account identifiers
      .distinct() // Get distinct account identifiers
      .filter(account => balancesMap.getOrElse(account, 0L) >= 100000000) // Filter accounts with at least one million euro
      .count() // Count the number of qualifying accounts


    println("OUTPUT: The number of accounts for Task 6 is %d".format(task6))


    /*
     * Task 7 (more challenging):
     *
     * ACME Bank pays interest to its 10000 accounts using a 
     * fixed interest rate of 0.1% per annum. The basis 
     * for the annual interest calculation is the daily
     * __minimum__ balance on each account. 
     * (The year 2014 partitions into exactly 365 consecutive
     * days, each 86400000 milliseconds long; cf. Task 5.) 
     * That is, for each day, we find the minimum balance on each 
     * account during that day, and then sum these minimum balances 
     * over accounts to get the interest payment basis for the day. 
     * Finally we sum over the 365 days in 2014 to get the total 
     * annual basis for interest payment, in euro cents. 
     * (The actual payment that ACME Bank makes then, in aggregate, 
     * at end-of-year to its accounts is the annual basis times 0.1% 
     * divided by 365; this, however, need not concern us.) 
     * In determining the daily minimum balance at an account, 
     * observe that every transaction is timestamped so that it takes 
     * place during a unique day. That is, the accounts affected by 
     * the transaction both have two different balances during the day
     * of the transaction. Namely, the balance before the transaction 
     * and after the transaction. The minimum of these two balances is 
     * the balance that contributes in the calculation of the daily minimum
     * balance for the day of the transaction.
     * 
     * Your task is to compute the annual basis for interest payment 
     * for the year 2014, in euro cents. 
     *
     * Write the CODE that computes the basis below.
     * Once you have the VALUE, save it in "transactionsSolutions.scala".
     *
     * Hint: all transactions have distinct timestamps. 
     *
     */

    val task7: Long = transactions
    .groupBy { case (timestamp, _, _, _) => timestamp / 86400000 } // Group transactions by day
    .values // Get only the transaction values
    .flatMap { dayTransactions =>
      dayTransactions
        .groupBy { case (_, account, _, _) => account } // Group transactions by account identifier
        .values // Get only the transactions for each account
        .map { accountTransactions =>
          val minBalance = accountTransactions.map { case (_, _, _, balance) => balance }.min // Find the minimum balance for each account on the day
          minBalance
        }
    }
    .sum // Sum the minimum balances over the 365 days
    .toLong

    println("OUTPUT: The basis for Task 7 is %d".format(task7))

conversation.content: As an AI language model, it's true that at lower temperature settings like 0.2, GPT-3.5 and potentially GPT-4.0 can output more conservative and repetitive responses. Low temperature values tend to make the model choose the most likely word or phrase at each step, which usually results in coherent but less creative text. It's important to balance temperature settings to achieve the desired level of creativity while maintaining coherence in the text generated.

Word statistics
This exercise asks you to use Apache Spark to study a (small) corpus of text available from Project Gutenberg, namely

An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations by Adam Smith
War and Peace by Leo Tolstoy.
(The files are provided in UTF-8 plain text and can be found in the data folder of this week's assignment.)
Remark. In this exercise we are working with a few megabytes of text (two books) to enable you to use your own laptop (or individual classroom computers) for the task. What you should realize is that from a programming perspective we can easily scale up the amount of data that we process into terabytes (millions of books) or beyond, without breaking sweat. All one needs to do is to run Spark on a compute cluster instead of an individual computer.

(See here and here for examples — these links are provided strictly for illustration only. Use of these services is in no way encouraged, endorsed, or required for the purposes of this course or otherwise.)

Remark 2. See here (and here) for somewhat more than a few megabytes of text!

Your tasks
Complete the parts marked with ??? in wordsRun.scala and in wordsSolutions.scala. Use scala 3.
Submit both your solutions in wordsSolutions.scala and your code in wordsRun.scala that computes the solutions that you give in wordsSolutions.scala.
Hints
The comments in wordsRun.scala contain a walk-through of this exercise.
Apache Spark has excellent documentation.
The methods available in StringOps are useful for line-by-line processing of input file(s).
This assignment has no formal unit tests. However, you should check that your code performs correctly on War and Peace — the correct solutions for War and Peace are given in the comments and require-directives in wordsRun.scala.

package words

  import org.apache.spark.rdd.RDD
  import org.apache.spark.SparkContext
  import org.apache.spark.SparkContext._


  @main def main(): Unit =

    /*
     * Let us start by setting up a Spark context which runs locally
     * using two worker threads.
     *
     * Here we go:
     *
     */

    val sc = new SparkContext("local[2]", "words")

    /*
    * The following setting controls how ``verbose'' Spark is.
    * Comment this out to see all debug messages.
    * Warning: doing so may generate massive amount of debug info,
    * and normal program output can be overwhelmed!
    */
    sc.setLogLevel("WARN") 

    /*
     * Next, let us set up our input. 
     */

    val path = "a01-words/data/"
    /*
     * After the path is configured, we need to decide which input
     * file to look at. There are two choices -- you should test your
     * code with "War and Peace" (default below), and then use the code with
     * "Wealth of Nations" to compute the correct solutions 
     * (which you will submit to A+ for grading).
     * 
     */

    // Tolstoy -- War and Peace         (test input)
    val filename = path ++ "pg2600.txt"

    // Smith   -- Wealth of Nations     (uncomment line below to use as input)
//    val filename = path ++ "pg3300.txt"

    /*
     * Now you may want to open up in a web browser 
     * the Scala programming guide for 
     * Spark version 3.3.1:
     *
     * http://spark.apache.org/docs/3.3.1/programming-guide.html
     * 
     */

    /*
     * Let us now set up an RDD from the lines of text in the file:
     *
     */

    val lines: RDD[String] = sc.textFile(filename)

    /* The following requirement sanity-checks the number of lines in the file 
     * -- if this requirement fails you are in trouble.
     */

    require((filename.contains("pg2600.txt") && lines.count() == 65007) ||
            (filename.contains("pg3300.txt") && lines.count() == 35600))

    /* 
     * Let us make one further sanity check. That is, we want to
     * count the number of lines in the file that contain the 
     * substring "rent".
     *
     */

    val lines_with_rent: RDD[String] = 
      lines.filter(line => line.contains("rent"))

    val rent_count = lines_with_rent.count()
    println("OUTPUT: \"rent\" occurs on %d lines in \"%s\""
              .format(rent_count, filename))
    require((filename.contains("pg2600.txt") && rent_count == 360) ||
            (filename.contains("pg3300.txt") && rent_count == 1443))

    /*
     * All right, if the execution continues this far without
     * failing a requirement, we should be pretty sure that we have
     * the correct file. Now we are ready for the work that you need
     * to put in. 
     *
     */

    /*
     * Spark operates by __transforming__ RDDs. For example, above we
     * took the RDD 'lines', and transformed it into the RDD 'lines_with_rent'
     * using the __filter__ transformation. 
     *
     * Important: 
     * While the code that manipulates RDDs may __look like__ we are
     * manipulating just another Scala collection, this is in fact
     * __not__ the case. An RDD is an abstraction that enables us 
     * to easily manipulate terabytes of data in a cluster computing
     * environment. In this case the dataset is __distributed__ across
     * the cluster. In fact, it is most likely that the entire dataset
     * cannot be stored in a single cluster node. 
     *
     * Let us practice our skills with simple RDD transformations. 
     *
     */

    /*
     * Task 1:
     * This task asks you to transform the RDD
     * 'lines' into an RDD 'depunctuated_lines' so that __on each line__, 
     * all occurrences of any of the punctuation characters
     * ',', '.', ':', ';', '\"', '(', ')', '{', '}' have been deleted.
     *
     * Hint: it may be a good idea to consult 
     * http://www.scala-lang.org/api/3.2.1/scala/collection/StringOps.html
     *
     */

    val depunctuated_lines: RDD[String] = ???


    /* 
     * Let us now check and print out data that you want to 
     * record (__when the input file is "pg3300.txt"__) into 
     * the file "wordsSolutions.scala" that you need to submit for grading
     * together with this file. 
     */
    
    val depunctuated_length = depunctuated_lines.map(_.length).reduce(_ + _)
    println("OUTPUT: total depunctuated length is %d".format(depunctuated_length))
    require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || depunctuated_length == 3069444)


    /*
     * Task 2:
     * Next, let us now transform the RDD of depunctuated lines to
     * an RDD of consecutive __tokens__. That is, we want to split each
     * line into zero or more __tokens__ where a __token__ is a 
     * maximal nonempty sequence of non-space (non-' ') characters on a line. 
     * Blank lines or lines with only space (' ') in them should produce 
     * no tokens at all. 
     *
     * Hint: Use either a map or a flatMap to transform the RDD 
     * line by line. Again you may want to take a look at StringOps
     * for appropriate methods to operate on each line. Use filter
     * to get rid of blanks as necessary.
     *
     */

    val tokens: RDD[String] = ???                        // transform 'depunctuated_lines' to tokens


    /* ... and here comes the check and the printout. */

    val token_count = tokens.count()
    println("OUTPUT: %d tokens".format(token_count))
    require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || token_count == 566315)


    /*
     * Task 3:
     * Transform the RDD of tokens into a new RDD where all upper case 
     * characters in each token get converted into lower case. Here you may 
     * restrict the conversion to characters in the Roman alphabet 
     * 'A', 'B', ..., 'Z'.
     *
     */

    val tokens_lc: RDD[String] = ???                        // map each token in 'tokens' to lower case


    /* ... and here comes the check and the printout. */

    val tokens_a_count = tokens.flatMap(t => t.filter(_ == 'a')).count()
    println("OUTPUT: 'a' occurs %d times in tokens".format(tokens_a_count))
    require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || tokens_a_count == 199232)

    /*
     * Task 4:
     * Transform the RDD of lower-case tokens into a new RDD where
     * all but those tokens that consist only of lower-case characters
     * 'a', 'b', ..., 'z' in the Roman alphabet have been filtered out.
     * Let us call the tokens that survive this filtering __words__.
     *
     */

    val words: RDD[String] = ???                        // filter out all but words from 'tokens_lc'


    /* ... and here comes the check and the printout. */

    val words_count = words.count()
    println("OUTPUT: %d words".format(words_count))
    require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || words_count == 547644)


    /*
     * Now let us move beyond maps, filtering, and flatMaps 
     * to do some basic statistics on words. To solve this task you
     * can consult the Spark programming guide, examples, and API:
     *
     * http://spark.apache.org/docs/3.3.1/programming-guide.html
     * http://spark.apache.org/examples.html 
     * https://spark.apache.org/docs/3.3.1/api/scala/org/apache/spark/index.html
     */

    /*
     * Task 5:
     * Count the number of occurrences of each word in 'words'.
     * That is, create from 'words' by transformation an RDD

     * pairs (c,w) such that w occurs exactly c times in 'words'. 
     * Then take the 100 most frequent words in this RDD and

     * a given answer for "pg2600.txt", the second question is

     *
     * Practice question for "pg2600.txt" (answer given below):
     * What word occurs exactly 1772 times in 'words' ? 
     * (answer: "pierre")
     *
     * The question that you need to answer for "pg3300.txt":
     * What word occurs exactly 777 times in 'words' ?
     * (give your answer in lower case)
     *
     */

    val word_counts: RDD[(Long,String)] = ???


    /* ... and here comes a check. */

    val top_word = word_counts.take(1)(0)
    println("OUTPUT: top word is \"%s\" (%d times)".format(top_word._2, top_word._1))
    require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || (top_word._2 == "the" && top_word._1 == 34558))

    /* ... print out the 100 most frequent words. */
  
    println("OUTPUT: The 100 most frequent words are, in rank order ...")
    word_counts.take(100)

Here is the completed code for wordsRun.scala file:

```scala
package words

import org.apache.spark.rdd.RDD
import org.apache.spark.SparkContext
import org.apache.spark.SparkContext._

@main def main(): Unit =

  val sc = new SparkContext("local[2]", "words")

  sc.setLogLevel("WARN") 

  val path = "a01-words/data/"

  val filename = path ++ "pg2600.txt"
  //    val filename = path ++ "pg3300.txt"

  val lines: RDD[String] = sc.textFile(filename)

  require((filename.contains("pg2600.txt") && lines.count() == 65007) ||
          (filename.contains("pg3300.txt") && lines.count() == 35600))

  val lines_with_rent: RDD[String] = 
    lines.filter(line => line.contains("rent"))

  val depunctuated_lines: RDD[String] = lines.map(_.filterNot(".,:;\"(){}".toSet))

  val depunctuated_length = depunctuated_lines.map(_.length).reduce(_ + _)
  require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || depunctuated_length == 3069444)

  val tokens: RDD[String] = depunctuated_lines.flatMap(_.split(" ")).filter(_.nonEmpty)

  val token_count = tokens.count()
  require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || token_count == 566315)

  val tokens_lc: RDD[String] = tokens.map(_.toLowerCase())

  val tokens_a_count = tokens.flatMap(t => t.filter(_ == 'a')).count()
  require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || tokens_a_count == 199232)

  val words: RDD[String] = tokens_lc.filter(_.forall(_.isLower))

  val words_count = words.count()
  require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || words_count == 547644)

  val word_groups: RDD[(String, Iterable[String])] = words.groupBy(identity)
  val word_counts_unsorted: RDD[(Long,String)] = word_groups.map { case (word, group) => (group.size.toLong, word) }
  val word_counts: RDD[(Long,String)] = word_counts_unsorted.sortBy(x => (-x._1, x._2))

  val top_word = word_counts.take(1)(0)
  require(!filename.contains("pg2600.txt") || (top_word._2 == "the" && top_word._1 == 34558))
  
  word_counts.take(100)
             .zipWithIndex
             .foreach(x => 
                println("Top %3d: \"%s\" with %d occurrences".
                          format(x._2+1,x._1._2,x._1._1)))
```

Radix-point decomposition of a rational number
This assignment asks you to study the properties of the radix point representation of rational numbers. In particular, the radix point representation of every rational number decomposes into three parts, each of which is a sequence of digits in the chosen base B:

the integer part
the fractional part, which further decomposes into
the finite transient
the finite period (that repeats infinitely many times)
This decomposition always exists and is unique if we choose the shortest possible finite transient, the shortest possible finite period, and require the period to be 0 whenever multiple choices exist for the period. In what follows we assume that the decomposition is always chosen in this way.

Example 1. Consider the rational number 45679/3700, which expands in base B = 10 radix point as 12.34567567567567…. Here the integer part is 12, the finite transient is 34, and the finite period is 567 (this repeats infinitely many times).

Example 2. Consider the rational number 23/1, which expands in base B = 10 radix point as 23.00000000000000…. Here the integer part is 23, the finite transient is empty, and the finite period is 0 (this repeats infinitely many times).

Example 3. Consider the rational number 3/2, which expands in base B = 10 radix point as 1.500000000000000…. Here the integer part is 1, the finite transient is 5, and the finite period is 0 (this repeats infinitely many times).

/* 
 * Description:
 * This assignment asks you to study the properties of the radix point
 * representation of rational numbers. In particular, the radix point
 * representation of every rational number decomposes into three 
 * parts, each of which is a sequence of digits in the chosen base B:
 *
 * (a) the integer part
 * (b) the fractional part, which further decomposes into
 *     (b1) the finite transient 
 *     (b2) the finite period (that repeats infinitely many times)
 *
 * This decomposition always exists and is unique if we choose the 
 * shortest possible finite transient, the shortest possible finite period, 
 * and require the period to be 0 whenever multiple choices exist for 
 * the period. In what follows we assume that the decomposition is always
 * chosen in this way. 
 *
 * Example 1: 
 * Consider the rational number 45679/3700, which expands in base B = 10
 * radix point as           12.34567567567567...
 * 
 * The integer part is      12
 * The finite transient is     34
 * The finite period is          567   (this repeats infinitely many times)
 *
 * Example 2:
 * Consider the rational number 23/1, which expands in base B = 10
 * radix point as           23.00000000000000...
 *
 * The integer part is      23
 * The finite transient is        (the transient is empty) 
 * The finite period is        0  (this repeats infinitely many times)
 *
 * Example 3:
 * Consider the rational number 3/2, which expands in base B = 10
 * radix point as           1.500000000000000...
 *
 * The integer part is      1
 * The finite transient is    5
 * The finite period is        0  (this repeats infinitely many times)
 *
 */

package rationalDecompose:

  /*
   * Task: 
   * Write a function that computes the decomposition of a given
   * nonnegative rational number p/q in a given base b. The decomposition
   * is to be returned in a three-tuple (integer, transient, period),
   * where each component of the tuple is a sequence (Seq)
   * of digits in base b, and each digit has type Int.
   *
   * Examples:
   * decompose(45679,3700,10) must return (Seq(1,2),Seq(3,4),Seq(5,6,7))
   * decompose(49,99,10)      must return (Seq(0),Seq[Int](),Seq(4,9))
   * decompose(3,2,10)        must return (Seq(1),Seq(5),Seq(0))
   * 
   */

  def decompose(p: Int, q: Int, b: Int): (Seq[Int],Seq[Int],Seq[Int]) =
    require(p >= 0 && q > 0 && p < 100000 && q < 100000 && b >= 2 && b <= 100) 
    // you may assume p, q, b meet the above requirements
    ???
  end decompose

Make sure your solution passess these tests:
package rationalDecompose

import org.scalatest.flatspec.AnyFlatSpec
import org.scalatest.matchers.should._

class rationalDecomposeSpec extends AnyFlatSpec with Matchers:
    "decompose" should "return the period part with the correct length" in {
        val test = Seq(((557, 12345, 10), 822),
        ((34567, 98765, 11), 19752),
        ((34567, 98765,  2), 3292),
        ((  567,   991,  2), 495),
        ((    1, 15017, 10), 15016))

        for ((p, q, b), answer) <- test do
            withClue("For p = %d, q = %d, b = %d, ".format(p, q, b)) {
                decompose(p, q, b)._3.length shouldBe answer
            }
    }

    "decompose" should "produce the correct results" in {

        ((98, 10, 10), (List(9), List(8), List(0))),
        ((123, 1, 10), (List(1,2,3), List[Int](), List(0))),
        ((45, 99, 10), (List(0), List[Int](), List(4,5))),
        ((34038, 275, 10), (List(1,2,3), List(7,7), List(4,5))),
        ((18245, 19998, 10), (List(0), List(9), List(1,2,3,4))))

        for ((p, q, b), answer) <- test do
            withClue("For p = %d, q = %d, b = %d, ".format(p, q, b)) {
                decompose(p, q, b) should equal (answer)
            }
    }

How we can write a vba to automatically detect elliot waves

conversation.content:
Elliott Wave theory is a complex technical analysis method developed by Ralph Nelson Elliott to predict future price movements by analyzing the historical price of an asset. Writing VBA code to automatically detect Elliott Waves requires a deep understanding of the theory and programming skills. Here's a step-by-step guide to create a basic VBA code to detect Elliott Waves in Excel:

1. Open Excel and press `Alt+F11` to open the Visual Basic for Applications (VBA) editor.

2. Click on `Insert` > `Module` to create a new module.

3. Start writing the VBA function to detect Elliott Waves. Define the function, variables, and input parameters. In this example, we will use an Excel range as input:

```vba
Function DetectElliottWaves(priceData As Range) As String
    Dim rowCount As Long
    Dim i As Long
    Dim waveCount As Integer
    Dim state As Integer
    Dim startPrice As Double
    Dim endPrice As Double
    
    'Get the number of rows in the input range
    rowCount = priceData.Rows.Count
    
    ' Initialize variables
    waveCount = 0
    state = 0
```

4. Iterate through the price data range to identify changes in the price trend that conform to the Elliott Wave pattern. Add a loop to analyze each cell in the input range:

```vba
    'Loop through each row in priceData
    For i = 2 To rowCount
        ' Get start and end prices for comparison
        startPrice = priceData.Cells(i - 1, 1).Value
        endPrice = priceData.Cells(i, 1).Value
        
        ' Identify trend changes based on price movements
        If endPrice > startPrice Then
            ' Uptrend
            If state <> 1 Then
                ' If trend has changed, reset waveCount and update state
                waveCount = 1
                state = 1
            Else
                waveCount = waveCount + 1
            End If
        Else
            ' Downtrend
            If state <> -1 Then
                ' If trend has changed, reset waveCount and update state
                waveCount = -1
                state = -1
            Else
                waveCount = waveCount - 1
            End If
        End If
        
        ' Check if it identifies with the Elliott Wave pattern (5 up, 3 down)
        If waveCount = 5 Then
            DetectElliottWaves = "Bullish Elliott Wave detected"
            Exit Function
        ElseIf waveCount = -3 Then
            DetectElliottWaves = "Bearish Elliott Wave detected"
            Exit Function
        End If
    Next i
```

5.Complete the function by returning the result when no Elliott Wave is detected:

```vba
    ' If no Elliott Wave is detected
    DetectElliottWaves = "No Elliott Wave detected"
    
End Function
```

6. Close the VBA editor and go back to Excel.

7. Test the VBA function by calling it in a cell and providing the range of your historical price data. For example, if the price data is in cells A2 to A100, enter `=DetectElliottWaves(A2:A100)` to get the result.

Please note that this example provides a basic approach, and the code may require further adjustments and improvements based on the specific financial data and desired level of complexity. Additionally, detecting Elliott Waves using real-world financial data often requires incorporating other technical analysis techniques and refining the rules for identifying the wave structure.

Tehtävä liittyy alle annettuun tietokantakaavioon, joka kuvaa erään yrityksen työntekijöitä, toimipisteitä, asiakkaita, projekteja ja sitä, miten työntekijät ovat kirjanneet työtunteja eri projekteille.
Relaatio Employees sisältää tiedot yrityksen työntekijöistä. Jokaisesta työntekijästä tiedetään yksikäsitteinen tunnus (attribuutti ID), nimi, työntekijän tuntiveloitus (asiakkaalta perittävä maksu jokaisesta työntekijän tekemästä työtunnista) ja sen toimipisteen tunnus, jossa työntekijä työskentelee.
Relaatio Branches sisältää tiedot yrityksen toimipisteistä (toimipisteen tunnus, nimi ja katuosoite) ja relaatio Customers yrityksen asiakkaista (asiakkaan asiakastunnus, nimi ja osoite). Relaatio Projects sisältää tiedot yrityksen projekteista (sekä meneillään olevista että jo valmistuneista). Projekteilla on numero ja nimi. Oletetaan, että jokaisen projektin on tilannut täsmälleen yksi asiakas, jonka tunnuksen kertoo attribuutti customerID. Attribuutti budget kertoo projektin suunnitellun budjetin arvon euroina.
Yrityksen työntekijöiden pitää päivittäin kirjata, kuinka monta tuntia he ovat käyttäneet aikaa mihinkin projektiin. Nämä kirjaukset tallennetaan relaatioon Reports. Attribuutti employee ID kertoo kirjauksen tehneen työntekijän tunnuksen, projectnumber sen projektin numeron, jolle kirjaus tehdään, date päivämäärän, jota kirjaus koskee, sekä hours sen, kuinka monta tuntia työntekijä on kirjannut tehneensä töitä tälle projektille ko. päivänä.
Tuntiveloitukset, tuntimäärät ja budjetit ovat desimaalilukuja. Muiden attribuuttien arvot ovat merkkijonoja. Voit olettaa, että attribuuteilla ei ole NULL-arvoja.
Tietokantakaavio:
Employees (ID pk, name, hourlyfee, branch ID)
Branches (BID pk, name, address)
Projects (number pk, name, customerID, budget)
Customers (customerID pk, name, address)
Reports (employeeID pk, projectnumber pk, date pk, hours)
Kirjoita seuraavat kyselyt SQL-kielellä:
a)
Kaikki Tapiolan toimipisteessä (toimipisteen nimi Tapiola) työskentelevät työntekijät, joiden tuntiveloitus on yli 100 euroa.
b)
Niiden toimipisteiden tunnukset ja nimet, joiden osoitteeseen sisältyy sana "Espoo" ja joiden työntekijöistä vähintään yksi on kirjannut tunteja projektille, jonka numero on PP-423.
c) Mille kaikille projekteille ovat krijanneet tunteja ne työntekijät, jotka ovat joskus kirjanneet työtunteja projektille, jonka numero on PP-423? Kyselyn pitää tuottaa näistä projekteista numero ja nimi.
d)
Haetaan asiakkaat, jotka ovat tilanneet yritykseltä projekteja yhteensä yli 500000 euron arvosta (saman asiakkaan tilaamien projektien budjettien summa on yli 500000 euroa). Kyselyn pitää tuottaa näistä asiakkaista asiakastunnus, nimi ja tämän asiakkaan yritykseltä tilaamien projektien lukumäärä.

Kirjoita seuraavat kyselyt relaatioalgebran lausekkeina:
e)
Niiden projektien numerot, joille joku Tapiolan toimipisteessä (toimipisteen nimi Tapiola) työskentelevä työntekijä on kirjannut työtunteja.

f)
Niiden projektien numerot ja nimet, joille vähintään kahden eri toimipisteen työntekijät ovat kirjanneet tunteja.

```sql
WHERE c.customerID = p.customerID
GROUP BY c.customerID, c.name
HAVING SUM(p.budget) > 500000;
```

Hamina (ruots. Fredrikshamn) on viehättävä ja historiallinen kaupunki Kymenlaakson maakunnassa, Kaakkois-Suomessa. Kaupungissa on noin 20 000 asukasta, ja sen pinta-ala on 1 155,17 neliökilometriä. Haminan kaupungilla on monipuolinen tarjonta kulttuurista, liikunnasta ja luonnosta kiinnostuneille, sekä erinomaiset palvelut niin asukkailleen kuin matkailijoillekin.

Historia

Haminan perustivat Ruotsin kuningas Fredrik I ja kenraalikuvernööri Viaporin linnoituksen rakentaja, paroni Augustin Ehrensvärd vuonna 1723. Kaupungin nimi tulee perustajiensa mukaan, sillä ruotsinkielellä Fredrikshamn tarkoittaa "Fredrikin satamaa". Haminan nykyään kuuluisa arkkitehtuuri ja sachsilainen leirintä periytyvät myös perustajilta.

Hamina on tunnettu linnoituskaupunkina, ja se on eräs Suomen vanhimmista kaupungeista. Haminan linnoitus, jonka nykyinen muoto on peräisin 1700-luvun loppupuolelta, on maailman ainoita täydellisiä pyöreitä linnoituksia. Linnoitus muodostui kahdeksasta bastionista, jotka ympäröivät koko kaupungin ja muodostivat tähtikuvion muotoisen ulkoasun yhdessä raveliinien kanssa. Tänä päivänä vain kolme linnoituksen bastioneista on säilynyt: Hollanti, Ruotsi ja Viro. Linnoitusalueella on useita vanhoja rakennuksia, muun muassa St. John's Church, joka on vihitty vuonna 1799, jolloin kaupunki jälleenrakennettiin isonvihan jälkeen.

Hamina on sotahistoriallisesti merkittävä paikka, jossa on käyty useita taisteluja. Tunnetuin niistä on Haminan rauhansopimus, joka solmittiin Turun rauhan jälkeen vuonna 1809, ja jossa Suomi luovutettiin Ruotsista Venäjän keisarikuntaan. Haminan rauhansopimus oli merkittävä virstanpylväs Suomen historiassa, koska se lopetti Suomen yhteyden Ruotsiin ja aloitti suomalaisen autonomian ajanjakson osana Venäjän keisarikuntaa.

Sijainti

Haminan kaupunki sijaitsee Suomenlahden rannikolla noin 145 kilometrin päässä Helsingistä itään. Haminasta on hyvät liikenneyhteydet Helsinkiin, Kotkaan ja Loviisaan. Merellinen sijainti antaa Haminan alueelle luonnollisen kauneuden, joka houkuttelee sekä asukkaita että matkailijoita.

Kulttuuri ja nähtävyydet

Haminassa on monipuoliset kulttuurinähtävyydet ja tapahtumat. Kaupungin vetonauloihin kuuluvat 1700-luvun lopulta peräisin oleva Ruutikellari, joka toimii nykyään konsertti- ja näyttelytilana, sekä 1800-luvulta asti käytössä ollut Haminan Teatteri.

Haminan historiallisessa keskustassa sijaitseva Pyhän Marian kirkko ja sitä ympäröivä hautausmaa ovat kulttuurihistoriallisesti merkittäviä kohteita. Kirkon sen jälkeen, kun Haminaan siirtynyt Hamina-Vehkalahden seurakunta irtautui Fredrikshamnin seurakunnasta vuonna 1950. Kirkon alttaritaulu ja saarnatuoli on veistetty 1700-luvulla.

Haminan kaupunginmuseo esittelee alueen rikasta kulttuurihistoriaa, ja Haminan laiva- ja vaakuna-arkisto tarjoaa mielenkiintoista tietoutta merenkulun historiasta Suomenlahdella.

Hamina Tattoo on kansainvälinen sotilasmusiikin tapahtuma, joka järjestetään joka toinen vuosi linnoituksen keskustassa. Tattoo houkuttelee sekä kotimaisia että kansainvälisiä katsojia ja esiintyjiä.

Luonto ja liikunta

Haminan kaupungin alueella on runsaasti luonnonkauniita alueita ja ulkoilumaastoja. Erityisesti merelliset maisemat tekevät alueesta idyllisen paikan ulkoiluun ja vapaa-ajanviettoon. Pyhäjärvi ja Vehkajärvi tarjoavat mahdollisuuksia veneilyyn, kalastukseen ja retkeilyyn, ja niiden rannoilla on useita laavuja ja tulistelupaikkoja.

Liikunnan harrastajia palvelevat monitahoiset liikuntapalvelut, kuten jäähalli, uimahalli, liikuntahalli ja keilahalli. Tämän lisäksi kaupunki tarjoaa erilaisia ulkoilureittejä ja kuntoratoja niin kesä- kuin talvikäyttöön.

Elinkeinot ja koulutus

Hamina on perinteisesti ollut vahvasti teollistunut alue, ja sen suurin työllistäjä on Haminan Satama, joka on yksi Suomen suurimmista satamista. Teollisuuden lisäksi Haminassa on panostettu palveluiden kehittämiseen.

Kaupungin koulutuksen tarjontaan kuuluvat useat päiväkodit, peruskoulut ja lukio, sekä ammattikoulut kuten ammattiopisto Tavastia ja ammattikorkeakoulu Xamk.

Yhteenveto

Hamina on rikkaan menneisyytensä, kiehtovan historian ja monipuolisten palvelujen ansiosta eräänlainen helmi Kaakkois-Suomessa. Se tarjoaa asukkailleen ja matkailijoille elämyksiä niin kulttuurin, luonnon kuin liikunnan saralla, ja sen satamatoiminta pitää alueen elinvoimaisena. Haminan kaupunki jatkaa matkaansa kohti uusia saavutuksia ja kehitystä, mutta sen historiallinen perintö säilyy aina sen arvokkaana ominaispiirteenä.

为什么老年人觉得时间过得快？

header.user-agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/113.0.0.0 Safari/537.36 Edg/113.0.1774.15

conversation.content:
老年人觉得时间过得快的原因有很多。以下是其中的一些原因：

1. 生活经验：随着年龄的增长，个体所经历的时间增多，每一年相对于整个生命历程所占的时间比例越来越小。因此，即使一年的时间总量不变，但在老年人看来，同样的时间在以前可能会觉得相对较长，而现在则觉得时间过得飞快。

2. 日常生活的重复性：老年人的生活往往相对较为固定，较少新鲜的事物和经历。在一种高度重复的生活节奏下，大脑对于外部刺激的关注减少，久而久之，时间在感知上会显得过得很快。

3. 记忆退化：随着年龄的增长，大脑的神经连接逐渐减少，记忆的形成和提取能力将有所下降。老年人可能较难回忆起过去一段时间内的具体情况，这使得时间在回顾时显得过得很快。

4. 生活节奏放缓：对于许多老年人来说，退休后的生活节奏会有所放缓。相对于年轻时的忙碌和充实，老年人可能会觉得时间变得空虚，但在回顾时，由于缺少具体的事物作为记忆锚点，也会觉得时间过得很快。

5. 心理因素：老年人可能会意识到自己所剩的时间有限，这种意识会让他们更强烈地感到时间在飞逝。此外，他们可能会更关注与时间相关的话题，从而让时间在感知上变得更快。

总之，老年人觉得时间过得快主要是由生活经验、日常生活的重复性、记忆退化、生活节奏放缓和心理因素等多种因素共同作用的结果。
Describe the responsibilities, members and methods of a scene class in relation to game engines

header.user-agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:109.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/111.0

conversation.content:
A Scene class is a fundamental component of game engines and plays a crucial role in managing and organizing various elements of the game. Here is an outline of its main responsibilities, members, and methods:

Responsibilities:

1. Organization: A Scene class organizes game objects, assets, and resources into a meaningful arrangement or hierarchy, helping developers manage the game world effectively.

2. Rendering: The Scene class is responsible for managing the rendering process of all visual elements, such as game objects, assets, textures, and lighting. This includes ensuring that objects are rendered correctly in relation to the camera and sorting objects by rendering layers or priorities.

3. Collision Detection: The Scene class often handles collision detection for game objects by maintaining a set of colliders and performing collision checks between them.

4. Event Handling: The Scene class may listen to and manage events generated by input devices, game objects, or other parts of the game engine. This includes passing events to appropriate objects, invoking event callbacks, and handling object updates.

5. State Management: The Scene class can manage scene transitions, scene activation or deactivation, and maintaining the current state of the game (such as paused, playing, or game over).

Members:

1. GameObjects: A list or container that holds all the game objects currently active in the scene.

2. Assets: A container or references to all the assets (models, textures, sounds, etc.) required to render the scene or interact with game objects.

3. Renderer: Responsible for rendering the visual elements of the game. This might include a dedicated rendering library, a graphics API (like OpenGL or DirectX), or a general-purpose API wrapper.

4. Physics Engine: A comprehensive system for handling the physics of game objects, such as collision detection, rigid body dynamics, and constraint simulations.

5. Event Dispatcher/Manager: A centralized system for handling and distributing events from input devices, gameplay systems, and other parts of the game engine.

Methods:

1. Load/Unload: Loading and unloading necessary assets and game objects when a scene is activated or deactivated.

2. Update: Updating the state of game objects and handling game logic during each frame. This might include updating the position and properties of game objects based on forces, animations, and user input.

3. Render: Executing the scene rendering pipeline, including setting up the renderer's state, submitting draw calls for all visible objects, and updating lighting and post-processing effects.

4. OnCollision: Handling the collision detection between game objects in the scene, invoking appropriate collision events, and applying the physical response.

5. HandleEvent: Receiving input events from an event system, dispatching them to appropriate game objects, and performing any-time sensitive actions based on user input.

These are just a few examples of the responsibilities, members, and methods that a typical Scene class might have in a game engine. The exact implementation details may vary based on the specific type of game being built or the particular architecture of a given game engine, but many of these concepts are common across a range of engines and game development platforms.
What's a good way to implement an input system in a game engine without using a singleton?

header.user-agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:109.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/111.0

conversation.content:
A good way to implement an input system in a game engine without using a singleton is to use the "Component-based" or "Event-driven" approach. Here's an outline of the two approaches:

Component-based Approach:

1. Create an InputComponent class, which can be attached to relevant game objects that require input handling.
2. The InputComponent class should define a set of virtual or abstract methods for handling different input events, such as OnKeyPressed(), OnKeyReleased(), OnMouseMove(), etc. These methods can be overridden in derived classes to provide custom input functionality.
3. Create a global InputManager class, which is responsible for polling the input devices (keyboard, mouse, gamepads, etc.) and managing the list of active InputComponent instances.
4. During each frame update, the InputManager should gather the input state from various input devices and then iterate through the list of InputComponents, calling their appropriate input handling methods based on the collected input data.
5. This approach allows you to avoid using a singleton, as the InputManager can be created and managed by the main game class or another global management class, and InputComponents are managed on a per-object basis.

Event-driven Approach:

1. Implement an InputEvent class that encapsulates the data associated with input events, such as key codes, button states, and modifier flags.
2. In your global InputManager class (created and managed by the main game class or another global management class), use a Publisher-Subscriber or Observer pattern to register input event listeners (subscribers) and dispatch input events to them.
3. When an input event occurs, the InputManager should create an InputEvent object with the relevant data and dispatch the event to registered listeners.
4. Interested game objects or systems can implement a Listener interface and register their event handling functions with the InputManager to receive and process input events.
5. This approach decouples input handling from game objects, enabling you to create modular, flexible input handling systems without relying on a singleton input manager.

Both approaches enable clean separation of input handling responsibilities and allow for increased modularity and extensibility in your game engine. Choose the approach that best meets the requirements of your specific game engine and development goals.

The script should keep in state the already given videos, so it never gives a duplicate video. The state can be kept in a text file next to the script.

header.user-agent: Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64; rv:109.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/113.0

conversation.content:
```python
import requests
import random
import sys
import os

def get_video_urls(playlist_id, api_key):
    base_url = "https://www.googleapis.com/youtube/v3/playlistItems?"
    video_urls = []
    next_page_token = None

    while True:
        request_url = (
            f"{base_url}part=snippet&maxResults=50&playlistId={playlist_id}&key={api_key}"
        )

        if next_page_token:
            request_url += f"&pageToken={next_page_token}"

        response = requests.get(request_url).json()

        for item in response["items"]:
            video_id = item["snippet"]["resourceId"]["videoId"]
            video_url = f"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v={video_id}"
            video_urls.append(video_url)

        next_page_token = response.get("nextPageToken")

        if not next_page_token:
            break

    return video_urls


def save_state(file, video_url):
    with open(file, "a") as f:
        f.write(f"{video_url}\n")


def read_state(file):
    if not os.path.exists(file):
        return []

    with open(file, "r") as f:
        return [line.strip() for line in f.readlines()]


def main():
    if len(sys.argv) < 3:
        print(f"Usage: python {sys.argv[0]} <playlist_id> <api_key>")
        sys.exit(1)

    playlist_id = sys.argv[1]
    api_key = sys.argv[2]
    state_file = "video_history.txt"

    video_urls = get_video_urls(playlist_id, api_key)
    video_history = read_state(state_file)
    available_videos = list(set(video_urls) - set(video_history))

    if not available_videos:
        print("No more videos available in the playlist.")
        sys.exit(0)

    random_video = random.choice(available_videos)
    save_state(state_file, random_video)
    print(random_video)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

To use the script, you need to provide the YouTube API key. You can obtain one by following [this guide](https://developers.google.com/youtube/registering_an_application). Also, you need to install the `requests` library by running `pip install requests`.

Then, run the script using the command:

```bash
python script.py <playlist_id> <api_key>
```

Replace `<playlist_id>` with the ID of the desired YouTube playlist, and `<api_key>` with your YouTube API key.

conversation.content:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

get_video_urls() {
    playlist_id=$1
    video_urls=$(youtube-dl -j --flat-playlist "https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=${playlist_id}" \
                 | jq -r '.id' | sed 's_^_https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_')
    echo "$video_urls"
}

save_state() {
    state_file=$1
    video_url=$2
    echo "$video_url" >> "$state_file"
}

read_state() {
    state_file=$1
    if [[ -f "$state_file" ]]; then
        cat "$state_file"
    fi
}

if [[ $# -lt 1 ]]; then
    echo "Usage: $0 <playlist_id>"
    exit 1
fi

playlist_id=$1
state_file="video_history.txt"

if ! command -v youtube-dl &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Error: youtube-dl is not installed. Please install it to proceed."
    exit 1
fi

if ! command -v jq &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Error: jq is not installed. Please install it to proceed."
    exit 1
fi

video_urls=$(get_video_urls "$playlist_id")
video_history=$(read_state "$state_file")
available_videos=$(comm -13 <(echo "$video_history" | sort) <(echo "$video_urls" | sort))

if [[ -z "$available_videos" ]]; then
    echo "No more videos available in the playlist."
    exit 0
fi

random_video=$(echo "$available_videos" | shuf -n 1)
save_state "$state_file" "$random_video"
echo "$random_video"
```

Before running the script, make sure you have `youtube-dl` and `jq` installed on your system. You can install them using the following commands:

For Debian/Ubuntu-based systems:

```bash
sudo apt install youtube-dl jq
```

For macOS (using Homebrew):

```bash
brew install youtube-dl jq
```

To use the script, run the following command:

```bash
bash script.sh <playlist_id>
```

Replace `<playlist_id>` with the ID of the desired YouTube playlist.

1. **Choose a Framework**: Select a back-end framework suitable for your preferred programming language (e.g., Express for Node.js, Django or Flask for Python, Ruby on Rails for Ruby, etc.). This framework will be used to create the server, manage routes, and handle incoming API requests.

2. **Design the API**: Define the routes, endpoints, and HTTP methods for the API. Here's a high-level overview of the API functions:

    - `GET /stocks`: Retrieve a list of available stocks.
    - `GET /stocks/{symbol}/price`: Get the current price of a specific stock.
    - `GET /users/{userId}/alarms`: Retrieve the user's stock alarms.
    - `POST /users/{userId}/alarms`: Create a new stock alarm for the user.
    - `PUT /users/{userId}/alarms/{alarmId}`: Update an existing stock alarm.
    - `DELETE /users/{userId}/alarms/{alarmId}`: Delete a stock alarm.

3. **Develop the Server-Side Logic**: Implement the server-side logic for the defined API endpoints, including stock price fetching, stock alarm management, and condition checking.

    a. **Integrate Stock Market Data**: Utilize a third-party API for fetching current stock prices (e.g., Alpha Vantage, Yahoo Finance, IEX Cloud, etc.). Implement functions for fetching and processing the stock data.

    b. **User and Alarm Management**: Develop a system for managing users and their stock alarms. This includes creating, updating, deleting, and fetching alarms.

    c. **Alarm Condition Checking**: Implement a function that periodically checks whether the user-defined conditions are met by comparing the conditions against the latest stock prices. If a condition is met, trigger an alert.

4. **Configure Data Storage**: Set up the database and data models for storing user information and stock alarms. Use appropriate database management systems such as PostgreSQL or MongoDB.

5. **Implement Authentication and Authorization**: Develop a user authentication system, ensuring that users can only access and manage their own alarms. Common techniques include OAuth2, JWT tokens, or cookie sessions.

6. **Develop Alert Notification System**: Implement a system for sending the alert notifications to users once the conditions are met (e.g., email, SMS, push notifications, etc.).

7. **Testing and Deployment**: Test the server-side application and API thoroughly, ensuring all components work correctly and securely. Once tests pass, deploy the application to a production server environment.

By following these steps, you'll be able to design and implement both the server-side communication and the API for your stock alarm system. Additionally, remember to ensure that the client-side application utilizes the API to perform and display user actions.

WRITE A CODE THAT MEASURES HEART RATE FROM 60-100 AND SHOWS THE DATA ON THE OLED SCREEN. ADD PIOTIMER AND ENCODER CAPABILITIES. HERE IS THE SOURCE CODE: import utime
import machine
from machine import Pin, Timer, ADC, I2C, PWM
from fifo import Fifo
import ssd1306

# Set up I2C, OLED display, and other pins
i2c = I2C(1, scl=Pin(15), sda=Pin(14))
oled = ssd1306.SSD1306_I2C(128, 64, i2c)
heart_rate = 0
peaks = []
peak_intervals = []

encoder_pin_a = Pin(10, Pin.IN, Pin.PULL_UP)
encoder_pin_b = Pin(11, Pin.IN, Pin.PULL_UP)
encoder_button = Pin(12, Pin.IN, Pin.PULL_UP)

led1_pin = 20
led2_pin = 21
led3_pin = 22

led1_pwm = PWM(Pin(led1_pin))
led2_pwm = PWM(Pin(led2_pin))
led3_pwm = PWM(Pin(led3_pin))

# Set up ADC and sampling
sampling_freq = 60  # Hz, default sampling frequency
sampling_period_ms = 1000 // sampling_freq
adc_pin = ADC(Pin(26, Pin.IN))
samples = Fifo(50)

# Set up timer for sampling at precise intervals
timer = machine.Timer()

def on_timer_tick(timer):
    sample = adc_pin.read_u16()
    samples.put(sample)

timer.init(freq=sampling_freq, mode=machine.Timer.PERIODIC, callback=on_timer_tick)

# Find peaks in the data
def find_peaks(data, threshold):
    peaks = []
    for i in range(1, len(data) - 1):
        if data[i] > threshold and data[i] > data[i - 1] and data[i] > data[i + 1]:
            peaks.append(i)
    return peaks

# Main program loop
window_size = 60  # 1 second of data at 60 Hz
window = []
prev_encoder_state = 0
encoder_state = 0
encoder_counter = 0
try:
    
    while True:
        # Read encoder state and adjust sampling frequency accordingly
        encoder_state = (encoder_pin_a.value() << 1) | encoder_pin_b.value()
        if encoder_state != prev_encoder_state:
            if (prev_encoder_state == 0b00 and encoder_state == 0b01) or (prev_encoder_state == 0b01 and encoder_state == 0b11) or (prev_encoder_state == 0b11 and encoder_state == 0b10) or (prev_encoder_state == 0b10 and encoder_state == 0b00):
                encoder_counter += 1
            else:
                encoder_counter -= 1
            encoder_counter = max(min(encoder_counter, 40), -40)
            sampling_freq = max(min(100, 60 + encoder_counter), 60)
            sampling_period_ms = 1000 // sampling_freq
            timer.init(freq=sampling_freq, mode=machine.Timer.PERIODIC, callback=on_timer_tick)
        prev_encoder_state = encoder_state

        if not samples.empty():
            sample = samples.get()
            window.append(sample)

            # Remove the oldest sample if the window is full
            if len(window) > window_size:
                window.pop(0)
            
            if len(window) == window_size:
                peaks = find_peaks(window, threshold=50)
                peak_intervals = [peaks[i+1] - peaks[i] for i in range(len(peaks)-1)]
            if len(peak_intervals) > 0:
                avg_peak_interval = sum(peak_intervals) / len(peak_intervals)
                heart_rate = 60 * sampling_freq / avg_peak_interval
        
        # Adjust the sampling frequency based on encoder input
        encoder_delta = 0
        if encoder_pin_a.value() != encoder_pin_b.value():
            encoder_delta += 1
        else:
            encoder_delta -= 1

        sampling_freq += encoder_delta
        if sampling_freq < 60:
            sampling_freq = 60
        elif sampling_freq > 100:
            sampling_freq = 100

        sampling_period_ms = 1000 // sampling_freq
        timer.init(freq=sampling_freq, mode=machine.Timer.PERIODIC, callback=on_timer_tick)
        
        # Draw the graph
        oled.fill(0)
        oled.text("Heart rate: {:.1f}".format(heart_rate), 0, 0)
        oled.text("BPM: {:.1f}".format(heart_rate), 0, 10)

        prev_x, prev_y = 0, 0
        for i, value in enumerate(window[-128:]):
            x = i
            y = 63 - int((value / 65535) * 32)  # Scale the sample value to fit the OLED screen
            if i != 0:
                oled.line(prev_x, prev_y, x, y, 1)
            prev_x, prev_y = x, y
        oled.show()

        if not peaks:
            print("No peaks detected.")
finally:
    print("Exiting program.")

```python
# Setup Encoder
encoder_pin_a = 10
encoder_pin_b = 11
encoder_button = 12
encoder = Encoder(encoder_pin_a, encoder_pin_b)

# Setup ADC and sampling
sampling_freq = 60  # Hz, default sampling frequency
sampling_period_ms = 1000 // sampling_freq
adc_pin = ADC(Pin(26, Pin.IN))
samples = Fifo(50)

# Setup timer for sampling at precise intervals
timer = machine.Timer()

def on_timer_tick(timer):
    sample = adc_pin.read_u16()
    samples.put(sample)

timer.init(freq=sampling_freq, mode=machine.Timer.PERIODIC, callback=on_timer_tick)

# Find peaks in the data
def find_peaks(data, threshold):
    peaks = []
    for i in range(1, len(data) - 1):
        if data[i] > threshold and data[i] > data[i - 1] and data[i] > data[i + 1]:
            peaks.append(i)
    return peaks

# Main program loop
window_size = 60  # 1 second of data at 60 Hz
window = []
encoder_counter = 0
try:
    while True:
        # Adjust the sampling frequency based on encoder input
        encoder_delta = encoder.get_steps()
        encoder_counter += encoder_delta
        encoder_counter = max(min(encoder_counter, 40), -40)
        sampling_freq = max(min(100, 60 + encoder_counter), 60)
        sampling_period_ms = 1000 // sampling_freq
        timer.init(freq=sampling_freq, mode=machine.Timer.PERIODIC_Callback=on_timer_tick)

        if not samples.empty():
            sample = samples.get()
            window.append(sample)

            # Remove the oldest sample if the window is full
            if len(window) > window_size:
                window.pop(0)

            if len(window) == window_size:
                peaks = find_peaks(window, threshold=50)
                peak_intervals = [peaks[i+1] - peaks[i] for i in range(len(peaks)-1)]
            if len(peak_intervals) > 0:
                avg_peak_interval = sum(peak_intervals) / len(peak_intervals)
                heart_rate = 60 * sampling_freq / avg_peak_interval

        # Draw the graph
        oled.fill(0)
        oled.text("Heart rate: {:.1f}".format(heart_rate), 0, 0)
        oled.text("BPM: {:.1f}".format(heart_rate), 0, 10)

        prev_x, prev_y = 0, 0
        for i, value in enumerate(window[-128:]):
            x = i
            y = 63 - int((value / 65535) * 32)  # Scale the sample value to fit the OLED screen
            if i != 0:
                oled.line(prev_x, prev_y, x, y, 1)
            prev_x, prev_y = x, y
        oled.show()

        if not peaks:
            print("No peaks detected.")

finally:
    print("Exiting program.")
```

In this code, I've added the Encoder object, which makes the code more concise and easier to understand. The main program loop now reads the steps from the encoder using the `encoder.get_steps()` function instead of reading the pin values directly. This reduces the complexity of the loop and helps improve readability.

```python
import machine
import ssd1306
import time
from machine import PWM, Pin, ADC

i2c = machine.I2C(1, scl=machine.Pin(15), sda=machine.Pin(14))
oled = ssd1306.SSD1306_I2C(128, 64, i2c)

adc = machine.ADC(machine.Pin(26))
adc.init(bits=12, atten=ADC.ATTN_11DB)  # Set the width and attenuation here.

buffer_size = 1000
buffer = bytearray(buffer_size)

while True:
    for i in range(buffer_size):
        buffer[i] = adc.read()

    ppg = sum(buffer) // buffer_size

    # Display the PPG value on the OLED screen
    oled.fill(0)
    oled.text("Heart Rate: ", 0, 0)
    oled.text(str(ppg), 0, 16)
    oled.show()

    # Wait for a short period to sample the PPG signal again
    time.sleep_ms(20)
```

In this updated code, I have removed the lines with `adc.width()` and `adc.atten()`, and replaced them with a single line to initialize the ADC object with the desired number of bits and attenuation. Everything else in the code should work as expected.

conversation.content:
The code you provided does not include any filtering, which is necessary for accurate detection of the heart rate from ECG or PPG data. The noise present in the raw data may cause the peak detection function to detect false peaks or miss actual peaks, causing incorrect heart rate values. One solution to this is to low-pass filter the data to remove high-frequency noise. Butterworth filters are commonly used to filter ECG/PPG signals. Unfortunately, MicroPython does not include a built-in function to apply a Butterworth filter. You may consider using a moving average or exponential moving average filter as an alternative.

Here's your code updated to include an exponential moving average filter with a smoothing factor of 0.1:

```python
import utime
import machine
from machine import Pin, Timer, ADC, I2C, PWM
from fifo import Fifo
import ssd1306

# Set up I2C, OLED display, and other pins
i2c = I2C(1, scl=Pin(15), sda=Pin(14))
oled = ssd1306.SSD1306_I2C(128, 64, i2c)

# ... (keep the rest of the code the same until the main program loop)

# Exponential moving average function
def exponential_moving_average(window, alpha=0.1):
    avg = window[0]
    for value in window[1:]:
        avg = alpha * value + (1 - alpha) * avg
    return avg

try:
    while True:
        if not samples.empty():
            sample = samples.get()
            window.append(sample)
            
            # Remove the oldest sample if the window is full
            if len(window) > window_size:
                window.pop(0)
            
            if len(window) == window_size:
                filtered_signal = [exponential_moving_average(window[:i+1]) for i in range(len(window))]
                peaks = find_peaks(filtered_signal, threshold=50)
                peak_intervals = [peaks[i+1] - peaks[i] for i in range(len(peaks)-1)]

                # ... (keep the rest of the code the same)

        try:
            utime.sleep_ms(sampling_period_ms)
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            print("Interrupted during sleep. Continuing...")

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Exiting program...")
```

While the exponential moving average may not be the ideal filter, it should help reduce noise and improve peak detection. If you still face issues with the accuracy, you may need to adjust the threshold value in the `find_peaks` function or consider using a more appropriate filter.

It appears that I accidentally missed copying the rest of the code, including the necessary functions and initialization, which is causing the code to not do anything. Here's the complete code with all the necessary functions and initializations:

```python
import utime
import machine
from machine import Pin, Timer, ADC, I2C, PWM
from fifo import Fifo
import ssd1306

# Set up I2C, OLED display, and other pins
i2c = I2C(1, scl=Pin(15), sda=Pin(14))
oled = ssd1306.SSD1306_I2C(128, 64, i2c)

# Set up ADC and sampling
sampling_freq = 250  # Hz
sampling_period_ms = 1000 // sampling_freq
adc_pin = ADC(Pin(26, Pin.IN))
samples = Fifo(50)

# Exponential moving average function
def exponential_moving_average(window, alpha=0.1):
    avg = window[0]
    for value in window[1:]:
        avg = alpha * value + (1 - alpha) * avg
    return avg

# Find peaks in the data
def find_peaks(data, threshold):
    peaks = []
    for i in range(1, len(data) - 1):
        if data[i] > threshold and data[i] > data[i - 1] and data[i] > data[i + 1]:
            peaks.append(i)
    return peaks

# Set up timer for sampling at precise intervals
timer = machine.Timer()

def on_timer_tick(timer):
    sample = adc_pin.read_u16()
    samples.put(sample)

timer.init(freq=sampling_freq, mode=machine.Timer.PERIODIC, callback=on_timer_tick)

# Main program loop
window_size = 250  # 1 second of data at 250 Hz
window = []

try:
    while True:
        if not samples.empty():
            sample = samples.get()
            window.append(sample)

            # Remove the oldest sample if the window is full
            if len(window) > window_size:
                window.pop(0)

            if len(window) == window_size:
                filtered_signal = [exponential_moving_average(window[:i+1]) for i in range(len(window))]
                peaks = find_peaks(filtered_signal, threshold=50)
                peak_intervals = [peaks[i+1] - peaks[i] for i in range(len(peaks)-1)]

                if len(peak_intervals) > 0:
                    avg_peak_interval = sum(peak_intervals) / len(peak_intervals)
                    heart_rate = 60 * sampling_freq / avg_peak_interval

                    # Draw the graph
                    oled.fill(0)
                    oled.text("Heart rate: {:.1f}".format(heart_rate), 0, 0)
                    oled.text("BPM: {:.1f}".format(heart_rate), 0, 10)

                    prev_x, prev_y = 0, 0
                    for i, value in enumerate(window[-128:]):
                        x = i
                        y = 63 - int((value / 65535) * 32)  # Scale the sample value to fit the OLED screen
                        if i != 0:
                            oled.line(prev_x, prev_y, x, y, 1)
                        prev_x, prev_y = x, y
                    oled.show()
                else:
                    print("No peaks detected.")

        try:
            utime.sleep_ms(sampling_period_ms)
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            print("Interrupted during sleep. Continuing...")

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Exiting program...")
```

This complete code should now work as intended, measuring heart rate and showing the data on the OLED screen.
